title: Multi-Stage Intrusion
briefing: Two hosts are lighting up within the same window. One external source appears to move from web requests into authentication attempts against $TARGET1, while a second, unrelated source hits SSH on $TARGET2. Reconstruct the timeline, separate the two actors, and decide which is the higher priority.
dashboard_hint: Widen the time range and sort by @timestamp. Correlate data.srcip against agent.name across both rule.groups: web and authentication_failed. Watch for one IP appearing in BOTH web and SSH alerts — that's your primary actor.

questions.prompt: List every attacking source IP you can identify (comma-separated).
questions.explain: $SRC1 hits web + SSH on $TARGET1; $SRC2 brute-forces SSH on $TARGET2.

questions.prompt: Which source is the PRIMARY (higher-priority) actor and why?
options: $SRC1 — moved from web exploitation to credential attack on the same host (kill-chain progression)
questions.answer: $SRC1 — moved from web exploitation to credential attack on the same host (kill-chain progression)
questions.explain: One actor progressing web -> auth on the same target ($SRC1 on $TARGET1) shows intent and chaining; a lone brute-force source is lower priority.

questions.prompt: What stages did the primary actor perform on $TARGET1? (select all)
questions.explain: Web SQLi + traversal, then pivoted to SSH auth failures — all from $SRC1 against $TARGET1.
The chained web+SSH activity all lands on $TARGET1.

questions.prompt: Severity for the primary actor's activity?
questions.explain: Multi-stage, single-actor, chained exploitation against one host warrants critical handling.

options: Hunt for successful web exploitation + new SSH sessions
Rotate credentials on the targeted host
answer: Hunt for successful web exploitation + new SSH sessions
Rotate credentials on the targeted host
questions.explain: Treat $TARGET1 as possibly compromised: block, isolate/snapshot, hunt for success, rotate creds. Blocking only the SSH source misses the primary actor.
